Previous market skepticism over AI - that major players were spending heavily but not making money - is dissipating and turning into belief. AI adoption is rising and revenue growth is accelerating.

We focus on a packed week of central bank decisions. We expect the Fed, ECB, BoE and BoJ to hold rates steady - though the latter is more finely balanced. Even if the BoJ stays on hold, we expect policymakers to signal more hikes are on the table this year. We watch the ECB and BoE decisions to see if they will look through energy-driven inflation rather than respond to the risk of it spreading into wider price pressures.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: Tesla: Why Q1 Results Were A Disappointment
data.description:
Overview

Having covered six of the Mag-7 in my previous article, I now move my focus to Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). Personally, I do not think there is anything magnificent about Tesla except the ridiculous P/E ratio it trades at despite the pathetic growth it continues to post. Whether it is some mysterious call-buying activity, retail investors believing the robotaxi/humanoid robot promises, the Elon Musk halo effect, or whatever else, it is fruitless to attempt to understand the reason for this monstrous P/E ratio.

In this article, I will first cover the Q1 earnings. After this, I’ll dive deeper into the automobile business, the energy business, and robotaxis. Tesla's Q1 numbers hide the underlying weakness of the business, which will likely become more visible to everyone in the coming quarters.

Q1 Financials: One-Offs Hide the Weakness

Tesla announced its Q1 financial results after the markets closed on 22nd April, mostly beating consensus estimates. The below image shows how Tesla’s financials fared against expectations and in comparison to Q1 2025.

I am slightly taken aback by the growth in revenues, considering that Q1 deliveries grew only 6% YoY and the energy business also experienced degrowth. Gross margin was also a major surprise. While these are all encouraging and bullish, it is important that we understand the drivers behind the results and reported beats.

Tesla earned an additional $0.9 billion in revenue due to favorable movement in foreign exchange rates. It also saved $0.2 billion in costs for the same reason. So, the net benefit from FX tailwinds was $1.1 billion, which is higher than the company’s operating profit, or EBIT, of $941 million. With the dollar index now flat YoY, it is unlikely that these factors will lift profitability in the coming quarter(s).

Another contributor to revenues is potentially SpaceX (SPACE) buying Tesla Cybertrucks. We know that SpaceX contributed $100 million to Tesla’s revenues in Q4 2025. It would not surprise me if that was also the case in Q1 2026.

Another contributor to Tesla’s profitability was some one-time or one-off benefits. Automobile segment gross profit improved due to "warranty true-downs around $230 million and some additional relief on tariffs." The energy business margins also benefited due to one-time benefits of $250 million tied to prior-quarter tariff recognitions. The below table shows Tesla’s gross profit and gross margins if adjusted for the one-time benefits.

Vertical Revenue COGS Gross Profit Margin (%) Adj. Gross Profit Adj. Margin (%) Q4 2025 Q1 2025
Auto 16,234 12,812 3,422 21.08% 3,192 19.66% 20.42% 16.2%
Energy 2,408 1,456 952 39.5% 702 29.15% 28.61% 28.75%

That’s another half a billion in profit that is unlikely to be earned in the next quarter in addition to the $1.1 billion that likely will not be earned in the coming quarter. Let’s not forget the revenue from the sale of credits is also on the decline. What these accounting anomalies do is distort the picture and hide the weakness in Tesla’s profitability.

A similar pattern is seen in the cash flow statement too. While Tesla's sales were down sequentially, the company's accounts payable grew further by $1.4 billion. Tesla's accounts payable have grown by $1.877 billion since the end of Q3 2025, in spite of largely flattish sales. These accounts payable will reverse at some point in the future, adding burden to the company's cash flow/liquidity position. Andre Thierig (Sr. Director of Manufacturing at Giga Berlin) announced that the company will increase Model Y output by 20% at the Gigafactory in Berlin. This could end up making things worse, as inventory has been draining cash flow for four of the last five quarters. Tesla's inventory has grown to 27 days of global supply from 10 days at the end of Q3 2025.

Tesla reported positive free cash flow for the quarter, but that was because CapEx was low at $2.5 billion compared to the company’s previous guidance of $20 billion, which was now revised upwards to $25 billion. As a result, the company expects to have negative free cash flow in the year. It remains to be seen whether this will be funded by debt (a burden on profitability and higher risk) or equity dilution or a mixture of both.

Business Segments:

Auto Business: Signs of Revival?

Whether retail investors accept it or not, the fact is that Tesla is an automobile company at the end of the day. Automobile revenues contributed to 73.3% of Tesla’s revenues and 72.3% of the company’s gross profit in 2025. In Q1 2026, auto revenues made up 72.5% of the company’s total revenues.

During Q1 2026, the company delivered 358,023 vehicles against consensus estimates of 365,000 to 370,000 vehicles. Deliveries declined 14% from the previous quarter but are up 6% compared to the same quarter, which is at least slightly encouraging as there was no decline, but the company needs a lot higher growth to justify the valuations.

To my surprise, the auto business was the bright spot in this quarter. For the company to have grown delivery volumes alongside some price hikes is undeniably a big positive. The newer, cheaper versions of the Model Y and the Model 3 appear to have been received well by the consumers. Higher oil prices created tailwinds for EV sales in Europe, and a weaker dollar (compared to Q1 2025) also helped create FX tailwinds from foreign sales, as the company would realize more dollars from the same number of foreign currency units.

Personally, I would want to see further acceleration in delivery volume growth and margin expansion before I become sure about whether this growth is a proper turnaround or a one-off quarter.

Energy: Weak Commentary in Q1

In the last couple of years, the automobile business failed to deliver strong growth, but the energy business has done a lot better. The below graph shows Tesla’s quarterly revenues from the energy business as well as the YoY growth.

However, concerns are emerging about whether this strong performance will continue. During the Q1 earnings call, CFO Vaibhav Taneja warned about increasing competition and the impact of tariffs. He said that the impact of tariffs is felt the most in the energy business, as most of the battery cells are imported from China. In my opinion, competition would not only hurt future growth potential but could also impact gross margins across the industry, and Tesla would not be shielded from it, in my opinion.

Since the energy business tends to be lumpy and less consistent throughout the years, I would yet again want to wait for a quarter or two before passing a clear judgement.

Robotaxi: Waymo Is What Bulls Think Tesla Is and Can Be

Tesla's unsupervised full self-driving, or the Tesla robotaxis, went from supposedly having no competition in the eyes of the bulls at one time to facing rising competition not just from Alphabet's (GOOG) Waymo but also from newer entrants such as Amazon's (AMZN) Zoox. Until not so long ago, the robotaxi was supposed to be the key contributor to the supposed value in TSLA that bears like me were said to be overlooking, even though I wrote articles with detailed calculations. Regardless, let’s dive deeper into what is happening on this front for Tesla.

Let me go through the positives first. Tesla began its robotaxi pilot in the city of Austin with a 20-square-mile coverage area, which has since grown to 245 square miles, for which it must get the credit it deserves. The percentage of unsupervised rides is 36% in the last 7 days and 25.7% in the last 30 days. What’s disappointing is that only 58 rides were done (or at least sampled) in the last week, out of which 21 were fully unsupervised.

However, even these cars are monitored remotely and are not fully autonomous. In October 2025, CEO Elon Musk told the All-In podcast that Tesla would have more than 500 autonomous vehicles in the Greater Austin Area by the end of the year. However, the actual number is under a tenth of that. As of the time of writing, out of 96 Tesla test vehicles in Austin, 51 have been inactive in the last 30 days. Out of 45, only 13 vehicles are being tested on a fully supervised basis. Additionally, the data shows that an unsupervised Tesla crashes once every 55,000 miles to 57,000 miles. This is anywhere between four and nine times more often than human drivers crash.

There is also a transparency gap when it comes to explanations for crashes. Waymo, Zoox, and others use the ‘narrative section’ to detail the cause of the crash, complex lane changes or intersections, and behavior of drivers in the other vehicle(s). This provides the regulatory body (NHTSA) and consumers with valuable information to understand the limitations of autonomous vehicles. Tesla, on the other hand, takes a very contrasting approach. The company always redacts its crash narrative section. While the company claims it is done to protect business secrets, in my opinion, it likely reduces the level of trust that the regulator and the public will have in Tesla’s unsupervised FSD systems.

The below image shows the fleet growth of Tesla’s robotaxis since it launched nine to ten months ago. Total active (seen at least once in the last 30 days) fleet is down from its peak of 234 on 8th March 2026 to 142 currently. The decline is in large part due to Bay Area operations, where the fleet has decreased massively from 189 on 8th March 2026 to 97 as of 23rd April 2026. The fleet in Austin, too, has nearly halved from its peak of 79 in late January 2026 to about 45 currently. These are not encouraging signs, especially when Waymo’s total fleet has doubled in the last year. Lastly, Elon Musk clarified that robotaxi revenues will grow to be materially significant only next year.

As for the competition, Waymo is already doing half a million rides every week. Elon Musk can ridicule and mock Waymo all he likes, but the ground reality is that they are years ahead of Waymo on the robotaxi. In fact, Waymo is what Tesla bulls think Tesla is. While the multiple sensor technology is more expensive, I still feel Waymo is more scalable than Tesla’s unsupervised FSD/robotaxi model. Waymo’s commitment and responsibility are limited to the sensors and the software, and so it can sell this package to almost anyone, after which the person can list their vehicle on a ride-hailing app or simply have it for personal use. Instead of dealing with individuals, Waymo can simply partner with other automobile manufacturers (Tesla’s competitors) and offer them the technology without having to worry about the manufacturing of the vehicles. I find it easier to envision a future where Waymo sensors and models will be on all cars, like the embedded Google Maps on automobile software systems.

However, Tesla is also responsible for manufacturing robotaxis, which lowers gross margins, and they need to crack the autonomous vehicle technology with far fewer detection devices (camera only, no RADAR/LIDAR). After that, they need to have sufficient control over the cost of the cameras and the regular bill of goods in manufacturing a car, which significantly lowers their margins and earning potential, in my opinion.

Conclusion

I would like to conclude by reminding investors that it is generally sensible to analyze earnings in some detail and not be moved by the headline/reported numbers. That is certainly helpful in analyzing Tesla's Q1 results, where a lot of one-offs hid the true weakness of the company.

I also discuss the outperformance of the automobile business, concerns around the energy business, and how investors might be over-optimistic around Tesla robotaxis.
